title: Courtier

infobox.value: ([/ˈkɔːrtiər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a person who attends the [royal court](/wiki/Royal_court "Royal court") of a [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") or other [royalty](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The earliest historical examples of courtiers were part of the [retinues](/wiki/Retinues "Retinues") of rulers. Historically, the court was the centre of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") as well as the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the monarch, and the social and political life were often completely mixed together.

["*Brokerage at the Court of Louis XIV*, by Sharon Kettering; The Historical Journal, Vol. 36, No. 1 (Mar., 1993), pp. 69-87; JSTOR"](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2639516). [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [2639516](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2639516). [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20160414004920/http://www.jstor.org/stable/2639516) from the original on 2016-04-14. Retrieved 2017-12-12.

Radner, Karen (2011). *The Oxford Handbook of Cuneiform Culture*. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press. pp. 358–379. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-19-955730-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-955730-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-19-955730-1").

Groß, Melanie; Pirngruber, Reinhard (September 2014). ["On Courtiers in the Neo-Assyrian Empire: ša rēsi and mazzāz pāni"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160303222502/http://iowp.univie.ac.at/sites/default/files/IOWP_gro%C3%9F:pirngruber_courtiers.pdf) (PDF). *Imperium and Officium Working Papers*. Archived from [the original](http://iowp.univie.ac.at/sites/default/files/IOWP_gro%C3%9F%3Apirngruber_courtiers.pdf) (PDF) on 3 March 2016. Retrieved 24 February 2015.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Person in attendance of a royal court

"Courtiers" redirects here. For the brush-footed butterflies, see [*Sephisa*](/wiki/Sephisa "Sephisa").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Robert_Dudley%2C_1st_Earl_of_Leicester%2C_Collection_of_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg/250px-Robert_Dudley%2C_1st_Earl_of_Leicester%2C_Collection_of_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester,_Collection_of_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg)

[Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester") was a courtier favoured by [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I").

A **courtier** ([/ˈkɔːrtiər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a person who attends the [royal court](/wiki/Royal_court "Royal court") of a [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") or other [royalty](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The earliest historical examples of courtiers were part of the [retinues](/wiki/Retinues "Retinues") of rulers. Historically, the court was the centre of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") as well as the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the monarch, and the social and political life were often completely mixed together.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtier&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Monarchs very often expected the more important nobles to spend much of the year in attendance on them at court. Not all courtiers were [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility"), as they included [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy"), [soldiers](/wiki/Soldier "Soldier"), [clerks](/wiki/Clerk_(position) "Clerk (position)"), [secretaries](/wiki/Secretary "Secretary"), agents and middlemen with business at court. All those who held a [court appointment](/wiki/Court_appointment "Court appointment") could be called courtiers but not all courtiers held positions at court. Those personal favourites without business around the monarch, sometimes called the [camarilla](/wiki/Camarilla "Camarilla"), were also considered courtiers. As social divisions became more rigid, a divide, barely present in [Antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") or the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), opened between menial servants and other classes at court, although [Alexandre Bontemps](/wiki/Alexandre_Bontemps "Alexandre Bontemps"), the head [valet de chambre](/wiki/Valet_de_chambre "Valet de chambre") of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), was a late example of a "menial" who managed to establish his family in the nobility.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The key commodities for a courtier were access and information, and a large court operated at many levels: many successful careers at court involved no direct contact with the monarch.

The largest and most famous European court was that of the [Palace of Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles") at its peak, although the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") was even larger and more isolated from national life. Very similar features marked the courts of all very large monarchies, including in [India](/wiki/List_of_Indian_monarchs "List of Indian monarchs"), [Topkapı Palace](/wiki/Topkap%C4%B1_Palace "Topkapı Palace") in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), [Ancient Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") or the [Caliphs](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") of [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") or [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"). Early medieval European [courts](/wiki/Court_(royal) "Court (royal)") frequently travelled from place to place following the monarch as they travelled. This was particularly the case in the early French court. But, the [European nobility](/wiki/European_nobility "European nobility") generally had independent power and was less controlled by the monarch until around the 18th century, which gave European court life greater complexity.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtier&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The earliest courtiers coincide with the development of definable courts beyond the rudimentary entourages or [retinues](/wiki/Retinues "Retinues") of rulers. There were probably courtiers in the courts of the [Akkadian Empire](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire"), where there is evidence of court appointments such as that of [cup-bearer](/wiki/Cup-bearer "Cup-bearer"), which was one of the earliest court appointments and remained a position at courts for thousands of years.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Two of the earliest titles referring to the general concept of a courtier were likely the *ša rēsi* and *mazzāz pāni* of the [Neo-Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire").[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [Ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") a title has been found that translates to [high steward](/wiki/High_steward_(Ancient_Egypt) "High steward (Ancient Egypt)") or great overseer of the house.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The courts influenced by the court of the [Neo-Assyrian Empire](/wiki/Neo-Assyrian_Empire "Neo-Assyrian Empire"), such as those of the [Median Empire](/wiki/Median_Empire "Median Empire") and the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), had numerous courtiers.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) After invading the [Achaemenid Empire](/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire "Achaemenid Empire"), [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") returned with the concept of the complex court featuring a variety of courtiers to the [Kingdom of Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(ancient_kingdom) "Macedonia (ancient kingdom)") and [Hellenistic Greece](/wiki/Hellenistic_Greece "Hellenistic Greece").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The [imperial court](/wiki/Byzantine_bureaucracy_and_aristocracy "Byzantine bureaucracy and aristocracy") of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") at [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") would eventually contain at least a thousand courtiers.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The court's systems became prevalent in other courts such as those in the [Balkan](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") states, the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Byzantinism](/wiki/Byzantinism "Byzantinism") is a term that was coined for this spread of the Byzantine system in the 19th century.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtier&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Examples")]

* [Bagoas](/wiki/Bagoas_(courtier) "Bagoas (courtier)")
* [Anne Boleyn](/wiki/Anne_Boleyn "Anne Boleyn")
* [Alessandro Cagliostro](/wiki/Alessandro_Cagliostro "Alessandro Cagliostro")
* [John Dee](/wiki/John_Dee "John Dee")
* [Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester](/wiki/Robert_Dudley,_1st_Earl_of_Leicester "Robert Dudley, 1st Earl of Leicester")
* [Anne Hungerford](/wiki/Anne_Hungerford "Anne Hungerford")
* [Princess de Lamballe](/wiki/Princess_Marie_Louise_of_Savoy "Princess Marie Louise of Savoy")
* [Dukes of Luynes](/wiki/Dukes_of_Luynes "Dukes of Luynes")
* [Henri Coiffier de Ruzé, Marquis of Cinq-Mars](/wiki/Henri_Coiffier_de_Ruz%C3%A9,_Marquis_of_Cinq-Mars "Henri Coiffier de Ruzé, Marquis of Cinq-Mars")
* [Louis de Rouvroy, duc de Saint-Simon](/wiki/Louis_de_Rouvroy,_duc_de_Saint-Simon "Louis de Rouvroy, duc de Saint-Simon")
* [Count of St. Germain](/wiki/Count_of_St._Germain "Count of St. Germain")
* [Cardinal de Rohan](/wiki/Cardinal_de_Rohan "Cardinal de Rohan")
* [Ja'far ibn Yahya](/wiki/Ja%27far_ibn_Yahya "Ja'far ibn Yahya")
* [Madame de Pompadour](/wiki/Madame_de_Pompadour "Madame de Pompadour")
* [Petronius](/wiki/Petronius "Petronius")
* [Walter Raleigh](/wiki/Walter_Raleigh "Walter Raleigh")
* [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu")
* [James Scudamore](/wiki/James_Scudamore_(courtier) "James Scudamore (courtier)")
* [Angelo Soliman](/wiki/Angelo_Soliman "Angelo Soliman")
* [Wei Zhongxian](/wiki/Wei_Zhongxian "Wei Zhongxian")

In modern English, the term is often used metaphorically for contemporary political [favourites](/wiki/Favourite "Favourite") or hangers-on.

## In literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Courtier&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In literature")]

In modern literature, courtiers are often depicted as insincere, skilled at flattery and intrigue, ambitious and lacking regard for the national interest. More positive representations include the role played by members of the court in the development of politeness and the arts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Examples of courtiers in fiction:

* [Rosencrantz and Guildenstern](/wiki/Rosencrantz_and_Guildenstern "Rosencrantz and Guildenstern") from [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")*.
* [Sir Lancelot](/wiki/Sir_Lancelot "Sir Lancelot") from [Arthurian legend](/wiki/Arthurian_legend "Arthurian legend"),
* [Gríma Wormtongue](/wiki/Gr%C3%ADma_Wormtongue "Gríma Wormtongue") from [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien")'s *[The Lord of the Rings](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings "The Lord of the Rings")*.
* [Count Hasimir Fenring](/wiki/Count_Fenring "Count Fenring") and [Gaius Helen Mohiam](/wiki/Gaius_Helen_Mohiam "Gaius Helen Mohiam") from [Frank Herbert](/wiki/Frank_Herbert "Frank Herbert")'s *[Dune](/wiki/Dune_(novel) "Dune (novel)")*.
* [Petyr Baelish](/wiki/Petyr_Baelish "Petyr Baelish") and [Varys](/wiki/Varys "Varys") from [George R. R. Martin](/wiki/George_R._R._Martin "George R. R. Martin")'s *[A Song of Ice and Fire](/wiki/A_Song_of_Ice_and_Fire "A Song of Ice and Fire")*.
